content.text: Every AWS region differs across six measurable dimensions: compliance certification, latency profile, service completeness, Spot capacity depth, pricing tier, and Direct Connect availability. Understanding each factor independently lets you score candidate regions against your workload's requirements — and explain the tradeoffs to stakeholders clearly.

attrs.alt: Region evaluation matrix: 6 major AWS regions scored across compliance, latency, service coverage, Spot capacity, pricing, and Direct Connect
attrs.title: AWS Region Evaluation Matrix

content.text: AWS maintains compliance certifications at the region level. Not every certification applies in every region — some are available globally, others only in specific regions, and a few (like FedRAMP High) require dedicated government regions. Your obligations come from three sources:
— legislation specifying which country or jurisdiction data must reside in (GDPR Art. 44–49, India's DPDP Act, China's CSL). These eliminate rather than rank regions.
— PCI DSS Level 1, HIPAA/HITECH, SOC 2 Type II. AWS holds these in most regions; your application layer must also comply.
— FedRAMP (US), C5 (Germany), IRAP (Australia), PASF (UK), ENS High (Spain). These require specific regions and sometimes separate partitions.
Checking What Certifications a Region Holds

# AWS Artifact (console-only for downloading compliance reports)
# Programmatically list available reports per region
aws artifact list-reports \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'reports[*].{Name:name,Category:category,Series:series}' \
  --output table

# Check if a specific compliance framework is active
# via AWS Security Hub standards
aws securityhub describe-standards \
  --region eu-central-1 \
  --query 'Standards[*].{Name:Name,Status:EnabledByDefault}' \
  --output table

The AWS Compliance Programs page at aws.amazon.com/compliance/programs lists current certifications per region. Treat this as the authoritative source — certifications are added as regions mature and audits complete.

AWS holds the certifications for the physical infrastructure and managed services it controls. You inherit the underlying controls but are responsible for everything in your application layer. For HIPAA, you sign a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with AWS; for PCI, you still submit your own Report on Compliance covering the application scope.
AWS Artifact lets you download AWS's compliance reports (SOC reports, PCI DSS Attestation, ISO certificates) to share with your own auditors. Access it via the console or the Artifact API — no cost.

Latency between a user and an AWS region is primarily a function of physical distance and fiber routing. The speed of light through fiber is approximately 200 km/ms. New York to Ireland (~5,500 km) implies a minimum one-way latency of ~27ms; actual RTT is typically 70–90ms due to routing overhead and processing.
Page load, API response time perceived by users
Synchronous replication, microservice calls across AZs
Typically <2ms; EC2 placement groups reduce it further
Direct Connect or VPN tunnel latency to AWS
Direct Connect SLA ≤5ms added for dedicated links
Measuring Latency from Your Users' Geography
Run this script from a machine representative of your users. For global products, deploy to a cloud VM in each major user city and compare results — what's fast from Sydney may be slow from Berlin.

AWS launches services in us-east-1 first, then propagates to other regions in waves. The gap between first availability in us-east-1 and a given region can range from weeks to years for niche services. More significantly, individual service features (not just services) roll out independently — a service may be present in a region but missing newer capabilities.
— new services launch here within weeks. These three regions receive virtually every service.
— follow within 1–3 months for most services.
— receive services 3–12 months after launch, and some services remain absent for years.
— separate release cycle entirely; 20–30% of services may be absent or require special approval.

# Check which regions offer a service via the SSM Parameter Store registry
aws ssm get-parameters-by-path \
  --path "/aws/service/global-infrastructure/services/bedrock/regions" \
  --query 'Parameters[*].Value | sort(@)' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1

# Check if a specific instance type is available in a region
aws ec2 describe-instance-type-offerings \
  --location-type region \
  --filters Name=instance-type,Values=trn1.32xlarge \
  --region ap-southeast-1 \
  --query 'length(InstanceTypeOfferings)'
# Returns 0 if not available, 1 if available

# List all supported regions for a specific Bedrock model
aws bedrock list-foundation-models \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'modelSummaries[?contains(modelId, `anthropic.claude`) == `true`].{Model:modelId}' \
  --output table

For architectures relying on newer services (Bedrock, Verified Access, VPC Lattice, Security Lake), always test feature availability in your target region with the AWS CLI before committing. The Regional Services page at aws.amazon.com/about-aws/global-infrastructure/regional-product-services/ provides a static snapshot but the CLI checks the live state.

Spot instances draw from the surplus capacity in each region's EC2 fleet. Larger regions have more physical capacity, which translates to deeper Spot pools and lower interruption rates. For workloads that use Spot (batch processing, ML training, stateless web tiers), region selection affects both availability and interruption risk.
Reading the Spot Instance Advisor Data

import urllib.request, json

# Spot Advisor publishes interruption frequency data as public JSON
URL = "https://spot-bid-advisor.s3.amazonaws.com/spot-advisor-data.json"
with urllib.request.urlopen(URL) as resp:
    data = json.load(resp)

INSTANCES = ["m5.xlarge", "c5.2xlarge", "r5.large"]
REGIONS = ["us-east-1", "us-west-2", "eu-west-1", "ap-southeast-1"]

# Interruption rate bands: 0=<5%, 1=5-10%, 2=10-15%, 3=15-20%, 4=>20%
RATE_LABELS = ["<5%", "5-10%", "10-15%", "15-20%", ">20%"]

for region in REGIONS:
    print(f"\n{region}:")
    region_data = data.get("spot_advisor", {}).get(region, {}).get("Linux", {})
    for inst in INSTANCES:
        if inst in region_data:
            rate_band = region_data[inst]["r"]
            savings = region_data[inst]["s"]
            print(f"  {inst:<15} interruption: {RATE_LABELS[rate_band]:<10} savings: {savings}%")

In practice, us-east-1 and us-west-2 show <5% interruption rates for most general-purpose instance types. Smaller regions routinely show 10-20% rates for the same types. For fault-tolerant workloads using Spot, spreading across multiple instance types and AZs in a large region beats deploying to a smaller region even at a slight price premium.

AWS sets prices independently per region based on infrastructure costs, power costs, taxes, and local economic factors. The price differences are not small: sa-east-1 compute costs roughly 40% more than us-east-1 for the same instance type. At scale, this difference compounds significantly.
Same instance family, same OS; all regions charge differently
eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1 are near parity for Standard class
sa-east-1 ($0.15/GB) vs us-east-1 ($0.09/GB); biggest regional delta
RIs are region-scoped; Savings Plans apply globally across regions
Hourly rate + per-GB processing; significant for high-throughput VPCs

# The Pricing API is only queryable from us-east-1 or ap-south-1
# Compare m5.xlarge on-demand price across regions
for LOCATION in "US East (N. Virginia)" "EU (Ireland)" "Asia Pacific (Singapore)" "South America (Sao Paulo)"; do
  PRICE=$(aws pricing get-products \
    --service-code AmazonEC2 \
    --region us-east-1 \
    --filters \
      Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=instanceType,Value=m5.xlarge \
      "Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=location,Value=${LOCATION}" \
      Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=operatingSystem,Value=Linux \
      Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=tenancy,Value=Shared \
      Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=preInstalledSw,Value=NA \
      Type=TERM_MATCH,Field=capacitystatus,Value=Used \
    --query 'PriceList[0]' \
    --output text 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "
import json,sys
d=json.load(sys.stdin)
od=list(list(d['terms']['OnDemand'].values())[0]['priceDimensions'].values())[0]
print('\$' + list(od['pricePerUnit'].values())[0] + '/h')
" 2>/dev/null)
  printf '%-35s %s\n' "$LOCATION" "$PRICE"
done

AWS Direct Connect provides dedicated network connections from your on-premises environment to AWS, bypassing the public internet. It delivers consistent latency (no internet congestion), higher throughput, and reduced egress pricing. Not every region has Direct Connect locations nearby, which matters for hybrid architectures.

# List Direct Connect locations for a region
aws directconnect describe-locations \
  --region eu-west-1 \
  --query 'locations[*].{Location:locationName,Code:locationCode,Provider:availableProviders[0]}' \
  --output table

# Create a hosted connection (partner-provisioned, for <1Gbps)
aws directconnect create-connection \
  --location EqLD5 \
  --bandwidth 500Mbps \
  --connection-name "HQ-to-eu-west-1" \
  --region eu-west-1

For regions with few Direct Connect locations, AWS Direct Connect SiteLink lets you use the AWS backbone to connect from one DX location to another without traversing the public internet. This extends effective DX coverage to regions that have no DX location of their own.
When multiple regions remain valid after compliance eliminates non-compliant ones, build a weighted scoring model. Assign weights to each factor based on what your workload needs most:

# Weighted region scoring — adjust weights for your workload
FACTORS = {
    "latency":      {"weight": 0.35},  # user-facing interactive app
    "service_avail":{"weight": 0.25},  # needs Bedrock + Trainium
    "price":        {"weight": 0.20},  # cost matters at our scale
    "spot_capacity":{"weight": 0.15},  # 30% of workload on Spot
    "dx_coverage":  {"weight": 0.05},  # minimal on-prem connectivity
}

# Score each factor 1-5 per region (based on measurement)
REGIONS = {
    "us-east-1":      {"latency":4,"service_avail":5,"price":5,"spot_capacity":5,"dx_coverage":5},
    "eu-west-1":      {"latency":3,"service_avail":4,"price":4,"spot_capacity":4,"dx_coverage":4},
    "ap-southeast-1":{"latency":4,"service_avail":3,"price":3,"spot_capacity":3,"dx_coverage":3},
    "ap-south-1":    {"latency":5,"service_avail":3,"price":3,"spot_capacity":2,"dx_coverage":3},
}

for region, scores in REGIONS.items():
    weighted = sum(scores[f] * FACTORS[f]["weight"] for f in FACTORS)
    print(f"{region:<20} score: {weighted:.2f}/5.00")

# Output:
# us-east-1            score: 4.85/5.00
# eu-west-1            score: 3.80/5.00
# ap-southeast-1       score: 3.50/5.00
# ap-south-1           score: 3.90/5.00  <- wins if users are in India

The weights shift the winner. A batch ML training workload (weight: spot_capacity=0.5, latency=0.05) produces a different winner than an interactive consumer app (latency=0.5, spot_capacity=0.05). Capture the actual weights in your ADR so the decision is auditable.

A subset of AWS regions require explicit opt-in before you can use them. Older regions (us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1, etc.) are enabled by default. Newer regions — particularly those in the Middle East, Africa, and parts of Asia — require manual enablement per account.

# List all regions and their opt-in status
aws ec2 describe-regions \
  --all-regions \
  --query 'Regions[*].{Region:RegionName,Status:OptInStatus}' \
  --output table

# Opt-in to a specific region
aws account enable-region \
  --region-name ap-east-1  # Hong Kong

# Verify opt-in completed (takes a few minutes)
aws ec2 describe-regions \
  --region-names ap-east-1 \
  --all-regions \
  --query 'Regions[0].OptInStatus'

Opt-in regions currently include: af-south-1 (Cape Town), ap-east-1 (Hong Kong), ap-southeast-3 (Jakarta), eu-south-1 (Milan), eu-south-2 (Spain), me-south-1 (Bahrain), me-central-1 (UAE), il-central-1 (Israel).
In an AWS Organizations environment, the management account must opt in at the organization level, not just the member account.
Enabling takes 5–10 minutes; some services require additional time before they become available.

Each AWS region is designed to be completely independent — power grids, network providers, physical facilities, and staff are separate. A region-level event (rare but real) cannot spread to another region. This independence is a key selection factor for disaster recovery planning.
— DR region should be on a different continent or seismic zone. Pairing us-east-1 with us-west-2 provides US-to-US separation; us-east-1 with eu-west-1 provides transatlantic separation.
— both primary and DR regions must satisfy the same data residency requirements. A GDPR-scoped workload cannot fail over to a non-EU region.
— the DR region must offer all services your application depends on. A service absent from the DR region prevents full failover.
— the two regions should not share the same undersea cable landing point or ISP peering. AWS regions are engineered for this, but your Direct Connect or VPN paths may not be.
Cross-coast separation; both have full service coverage
GDPR-compliant pair; different countries, different power grids
Singapore + Tokyo; both mature APAC regions with broad service coverage
Both within India for RBI compliance; different cities, different grid zones

Older regions have deeper operational history: more Support engineers in that timezone, more APN partner solutions tested against that region, more EC2 instance families available, and a longer track record of availability. Newer regions may lack some of these softer advantages.
All families incl. bare-metal, GPU, HPC
Fewer tested listings; some partners not yet certified
May route to nearest team timezone
Sparse; region-specific issues may lack documentation

For teams managing multi-region infrastructure, automate region selection validation as part of the deployment pipeline. The following checks ensure deployments only proceed to pre-approved, service-complete, compliant regions:
Embed this check as a pre-deployment step in your CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions, CodePipeline) so that misconfigured target regions are caught before any infrastructure is provisioned.
Reserved Instances and Savings Plans: Regional Considerations
Compute commitments behave differently across regions, and understanding this affects the economics of multi-region deployments significantly.
are scoped to a specific region and instance type family. An RI purchased in eu-west-1 provides no discount in eu-central-1. Buying RIs in the wrong region wastes the commitment.

apply across all regions, all instance families, and all OS types. This makes them ideal for multi-region architectures where workloads shift between regions. The savings rate is slightly lower than Standard RIs (~66% vs ~72%) in exchange for flexibility.
apply within a specific region but across all sizes in a family. They offer higher savings (~72%) while still allowing flexibility in instance size.

# Analyze savings plan coverage and utilization across all regions
aws ce get-savings-plans-utilization \
  --time-period Start=2024-01-01,End=2024-12-31 \
  --granularity MONTHLY \
  --filter '{
    "Dimensions": {
      "Key": "REGION",
      "Values": ["eu-west-1", "eu-central-1"]
    }
  }' \
  --query 'SavingsPlansUtilizationsByTime[*].{Month:TimePeriod.Start,Utilization:Total.UtilizationPercentage}' \
  --output table

# Check reserved instance coverage breakdown by region
aws ce get-reservation-coverage \
  --time-period Start=2024-01-01,End=2024-12-31 \
  --group-by Type=DIMENSION,Key=REGION \
  --query 'CoveragesByTime[0].Groups[*].{Region:Keys[0],Coverage:Coverage.CoverageHours.CoverageHoursPercentage}' \
  --output table
RI Scope Portability vs. Price Commitment
Stable load in one region, flexible size
Single region, single AZ or region
Steady-state, known instance type, single region
Six factors determine region fit: compliance certifications, latency profile, service completeness, Spot capacity depth, pricing tier, and Direct Connect availability.
Compliance constraints are binary eliminators, not scores — non-compliant regions are removed from consideration before any scoring begins.
Service completeness follows a launch-wave model; us-east-1 and us-west-2 receive every new service first, smaller regions lag by months to years.
Use a weighted scoring model to make the region decision quantitative and auditable — vary weights based on whether latency, cost, or Spot capacity dominates your workload's requirements.
Some newer regions require opt-in at the account or organization level before resources can be deployed there.
For hybrid architectures, Direct Connect location density is a practical constraint — regions with few DX locations impose higher connection costs and fewer carrier choices.
